card.intuition: Visit Left, then Right, then Root — children are always processed before their parent, making it natural for bottom-up computations.
card.analogy: Cleaning up after a party: you tidy every room in the east wing, then the west wing, then the hallway between them — always finish sub-areas before the connecting space.
when_to_use: Delete a tree — you must free children before the parent
Evaluate an expression tree — compute leaves before operators
Tree DP where a node's answer depends on its children's answers
Compute subtree sizes, depths, or any aggregate that propagates upward
gotchas: Iterative post-order trick: run a modified pre-order (Root, Right, Left) then reverse the result — gives Left, Right, Root
Two-stack approach: push to result stack instead of printing; pop result stack at the end
Don't confuse with pre-order — post-order root comes LAST, pre-order root comes FIRST

exercise.prompt: Implement iterative post-order using the 'reverse modified pre-order' trick.
hints: This modified pre-order pushes left first (so right is processed first), giving Root→Right→Left order
Reversing Root→Right→Left gives Left→Right→Root, which is post-order
exercise.test_code:
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self,v,l=None,r=None): self.val=v;self.left=l;self.right=r
root=TreeNode(1,TreeNode(2,TreeNode(4),TreeNode(5)),TreeNode(3))
assert postorder(root) == [4,5,2,3,1], 'Standard post-order'
assert postorder(TreeNode(1)) == [1], 'Single'
assert postorder(None) == [], 'Empty'
root2=TreeNode(1,TreeNode(2,TreeNode(3)))
assert postorder(root2) == [3,2,1], 'Left-skewed'
print('PASS')
